# Emerging Targets and Novel Agents

## CELMoDs: Next-Generation Cereblon Modulators

Cereblon E3 ligase modulatory drugs (CELMoDs) represent an evolution beyond conventional immunomodulatory drugs (IMiDs), engineered for enhanced binding affinity to cereblon and more potent degradation of Ikaros (IKZF1) and Aeros (IKZF3). Mezigdomide (CC-92480) has demonstrated activity in patients refractory to lenalidomide and pomalidomide, achieving an ORR of approximately 40% in triple-class-refractory populations when combined with dexamethasone. The augmented cereblon binding of mezigdomide enables it to overcome a subset of CRBN pathway mutations that confer resistance to earlier-generation IMiDs, although complete loss of cereblon expression remains a barrier [@Kortm2016Targeted]. Iberdomide (CC-220), another CELMoD, has shown similar promise in early-phase trials with ORRs of 30-40% in heavily pretreated patients. Both agents are now being evaluated in combination with anti-CD38 antibodies, proteasome inhibitors, and bispecific antibodies, with the expectation that CELMoD-based combinations will extend the utility of cereblon-directed pharmacology into later lines of therapy [@Rajkumar2022Multiple].

## GPRC5D-Directed Therapies

G protein-coupled receptor, class C, group 5, member D (GPRC5D) is an orphan receptor highly expressed on malignant plasma cells with limited expression on normal tissues, making it an attractive immunotherapeutic target independent of BCMA. Talquetamab, the most advanced GPRC5D-directed agent, is a bispecific antibody redirecting T cells to GPRC5D-expressing myeloma cells. In the phase 1 MonumenTAL-1 study, talquetamab achieved ORRs of 70% and 64% at the two recommended phase 2 doses (405 mcg/kg weekly and 800 mcg/kg biweekly, respectively) in patients with a median of six prior lines of therapy [@Chari2022Talquetamab]. The unique toxicity profile of talquetamab, including skin-related events (67-70%), dysgeusia (57-63%), and nail changes, reflects GPRC5D expression in keratinocytes and taste buds. Critically, GPRC5D-directed therapies retain activity following failure of BCMA-directed treatments, positioning them as sequential options in a rational immunotherapy treatment pathway. GPRC5D-targeting CAR T-cell products are also in clinical development, with early data suggesting high response rates and manageable toxicity in BCMA-refractory populations.

## FcRH5 (FCRLA/5) as a Novel Target

Fc receptor-homolog 5 (FcRH5, also known as FCRLA/5) is expressed on B cells and plasma cells and represents another non-BCMA target under active investigation. Cevostamab, a bispecific antibody targeting FcRH5 and CD3, has demonstrated an ORR of approximately 37% in patients with triple-class-refractory RRMM, including patients who had received prior BCMA-directed therapy. This target diversification is essential for addressing antigen escape and sequential immunotherapy resistance, as relapse following BCMA-directed CAR T cells or bispecific antibodies may involve BCMA downregulation or loss [@Samur2021Biallelic]. The development of therapies against multiple distinct plasma cell surface antigens enables the construction of sequential or combinatorial immunotherapy strategies designed to minimize the probability of antigen escape.

## Venetoclax for t(11;14) Multiple Myeloma

Venetoclax, a selective oral BCL-2 inhibitor, exploits a biologically defined vulnerability in the subset of myeloma harboring the t(11;14) translocation, which results in cyclin D1 overexpression and high BCL-2-to-BCL-XL ratios. Kumar and colleagues demonstrated that venetoclax monotherapy achieved an ORR of 40% in t(11;14)-positive RRMM, with several patients attaining deep responses including stringent complete responses [@Kumar2017Efficacy]. In combination with dexamethasone, response rates were further enhanced, and the addition of a proteasome inhibitor (bortezomib) expanded the responsive population beyond the t(11;14) subgroup. However, a phase 3 trial of venetoclax-bortezomib-dexamethasone versus placebo-bortezomib-dexamethasone (BELLINI) revealed an excess mortality risk in the unselected population, driven by increased infections. Subsequent biomarker-driven analyses confirmed that the survival benefit was confined to patients with t(11;14) or high BCL-2 expression, establishing venetoclax as a paradigm for molecularly selected therapy in myeloma [@Rajkumar2022Multiple]. This approach exemplifies the broader move toward precision medicine in RRMM treatment selection.

## Selinexor: Nuclear Export Inhibition

Selinexor, a first-in-class selective inhibitor of nuclear export (SINE) that blocks XPO1 (exportin 1), traps tumor suppressor proteins and growth regulators in the nucleus, inducing myeloma cell apoptosis. The phase 2 STORM trial evaluated selinexor plus dexamethasone in 122 patients with penta-refractory myeloma (refractory to bortezomib, carfilzomib, lenalidomide, pomalidomide, and daratumumab). The ORR was 26% (including 2 patients with stringent CR), with a median PFS of 3.7 months and median OS of 8.6 months [@Chari2019Oral]. Despite the modest single-agent activity, selinexor represented the first therapy to demonstrate meaningful responses in the penta-refractory population. Common grade 3-4 adverse events included thrombocytopenia (59%), fatigue (25%), and nausea (22%), managed with supportive care modifications.

The phase 3 BOSTON trial compared selinexor plus bortezomib and dexamethasone (SVd) versus bortezomib-dexamethasone (Vd) in 402 patients with RRMM after one to three prior lines. SVd achieved a median PFS of 13.9 months versus 9.5 months for Vd (HR 0.70; 95% CI, 0.53-0.93; P = 0.0075). Importantly, once-weekly bortezomib dosing in the SVd arm reduced peripheral neuropathy while maintaining efficacy, offering a practical advantage over twice-weekly bortezomib schedules [@Rajkumar2022Multiple].

## Combination Strategies and Sequencing Challenges

The expanding therapeutic arsenal for RRMM introduces unprecedented complexity in treatment sequencing. Several principles are emerging to guide rational therapy selection. First, combinations exploiting non-overlapping resistance mechanisms maximize the probability of deep response: the pairing of CELMoDs with bispecific antibodies, for example, combines enhanced immunomodulation with T-cell redirection [@Dimopoulos2021Multiple]. Second, sequential use of immunotherapies targeting distinct antigens (BCMA followed by GPRC5D, or vice versa) may circumvent antigen-directed resistance, though optimal sequencing remains empirically undefined.

Triple-class-refractory patients who have exhausted PI, IMiD, and anti-CD38 therapies face the most challenging treatment decisions. In this population, median OS is approximately 8.6 months with conventional salvage regimens [@Gandhi2019Outcomes]. BCMA-directed CAR T cells and bispecific antibodies have dramatically improved outcomes for these patients, but the availability of multiple effective immunotherapeutic options raises the question of whether CAR T cells or bispecific antibodies should be deployed first. Practical considerations including manufacturing timelines for CAR T products, infectious toxicity profiles, and the finite duration of bispecific antibody responses all influence this decision [@Cappell2023Longterm].

Emerging combination strategies seek to overcome these limitations by pairing immunotherapies with agents that enhance immune function or target the myeloma microenvironment. Combinations of bispecific antibodies with IMiDs or CELMoDs aim to augment T-cell fitness and reduce exhaustion, while combinations with anti-CD38 antibodies leverage complementary mechanisms of immune-mediated killing. The integration of checkpoint inhibitors, which showed limited single-agent activity in myeloma [@Lesokhin2016Nivolumab], is being revisited in the context of combination with T-cell-redirecting therapies to counteract the immunosuppressive microenvironment.

As frontline therapy increasingly incorporates anti-CD38 antibodies and quadruplet induction regimens, the population of patients relapsing with multi-drug-refractory disease will continue to grow. Clinical trials evaluating early integration of immunotherapies, including CAR T cells in first relapse [@SanMiguel2023Ciltacel; @RodriguezOtero2023Idecel], are challenging the traditional paradigm of reserving these agents for later lines. The ongoing evolution of the treatment landscape demands adaptive clinical trial designs, biomarker-driven selection strategies, and real-world evidence generation to optimize outcomes for patients across the continuum of relapsed and refractory multiple myeloma [@van2021Multiple].
